title: Defect-Engineered Beryllium Dinitride (BeN2) Monolayer with Light-Metal Decoration for Reversible High-Capacity Hydrogen Storage
authors: Wael Othman, Ibrahim Alghoul, K-F. Aguey-Zinsou, Nacir Tit, Tanveer Hussain
abstract: Hydrogen (H2) possesses the highest gravimetric energy density of any chemical fuel and is the most abundant element in the universe. However, its extremely low volumetric energy density at standard conditions imposes a fundamental materials challenge for safe, efficient, and reversible storage. Here, we report a defect-engineered 2D beryllium dinitride (BeN2) monolayer that enables stable light-metal functionalization for high-capacity H2 storage. A 2 x 2 supercell containing two intrinsic beryllium vacancies accommodates four Li, Na, and K atoms without clustering, exhibiting strong average metal-vacancy binding energies of -3.80, -2.94, and -3.18 eV, respectively. Ab initio molecular dynamics simulations at 400 K confirm the thermal stability of the metal-decorated frameworks and the suppression of metal aggregation. The vacancy-stabilized alkali-metal centers generate localized charge polarization that facilitates the adsorption of up to 20 H2 molecules per supercell, with average adsorption energies of -0.182 eV (Li), -0.191 eV (Na), and -0.171 eV (K), making the adsorption reversible under near-ambient conditions. The corresponding gravimetric H2 storage capacities reach 11.64, 9.82, and 8.49 wt percent, respectively, significantly exceeding the US Department of Energy (DOE) ultimate target of 6.50 wt percent. Moreover, thermodynamic analysis further confirms favorable adsorption-desorption behavior within practical operating windows. These results establish vacancy-defected light-metal decorated BeN2 as a viable design strategy for high-density, reversible H2 storage, providing a scalable framework for engineering polar lightweight materials for energy storage applications.
summary: The study proposes a method to engineer 2D BeN2 monolayers with beryllium vacancies and alkali metal decorations to create a high-capacity, reversible hydrogen storage medium. The researchers demonstrate that the Li-decorated system can exceed DOE-mandated storage targets under near-ambient conditions. This provides a scalable design strategy for using polar lightweight materials in energy storage applications.
main_problem: The challenge of developing a reversible, high-capacity hydrogen storage material that meets the US Department of Energy (DOE) target of 6.50 wt% while avoiding the safety risks of high-pressure or cryogenic storage.
main_result: A defect-engineered BeN2 monolayer decorated with Li, Na, or K atoms enables high-capacity hydrogen storage, with the Li-decorated system achieving an effective reversible capacity of 7.69 wt%, exceeding the DOE target.
method: Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the VASP package, Ab Initio Molecular Dynamics (AIMD) at 400 K, Bader charge analysis, and statistical thermodynamic modeling using the grand canonical partition function.
model_or_system: A 2D beryllium dinitride (BeN2) monolayer containing beryllium vacancies (BeN2:2VBe) functionalized with light alkali metal atoms (Li, Na, or K) and interacting with H2 molecules.
key_observables: Gravimetric hydrogen storage capacity (wt%), adsorption energy (Eads), metal-vacancy binding energy (Ebind), and electronic band gap (Eg).
important_parameters: Temperature (400 K for stability, 25 C/100 C for adsorption/desorption), pressure (30 atm/3 atm), and the critical adsorption energy threshold of 0.15 eV.
main_assumptions: Use of the GGA-PBE functional with DFT-D3 corrections; use of the Born-Oppenheimer frozen-lattice approximation for theoretical capacity calculations at 0 K.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows atomic structures and electronic band structures/DOS; Figure 7 shows the trend of adsorption energy vs. H2 number and theoretical capacities; Figure 8 presents an N-P-T phase diagram for hydrogen molecules.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the hydrogen storage problem, describes the structural design of the defect-engineered BeN2, details the computational methods, presents results on stability and electronic properties, analyzes hydrogen adsorption energetics and capacity, and concludes with thermodynamic performance under practical conditions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Optimized atomic structures (top and side views) and (b) spin-polarized electronic band structure with corresponding projected/total density of states (PDOS/TDOS) of (i) pristine BeN2 and (ii) vacancy-defected BeN2 (BeN2:2VBe). The primitive cell of BeN2 contains 2 Be and 4 N atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice with lattice parameters a = b = 4.541 Å and c = 20 Å (vacuum spacing), α = β = 90°, and γ = 120°. The 2×2 supercell containing two Be vacancies (BeN2:2VBe) consists of 6 Be and 16 N atoms with lattice parameters a = b = 9.081 Å. Pristine BeN2 exhibits a direct band gap of 1.34 eV with a non-magnetic ground state, whereas vacancy engineering induces metallic behavior...

Figure 2. Optimized structures (top and side views) of (a) 4Li@BeN2:2VBe, (b) 4Na@BeN2:2VBe, and (c) 4K@BeN2:2VBe monolayers. AIMD simulations at 400 K confirm the thermal stability of the light- metal-functionalized BeN2:2VBe monolayers.

Figure 3. (a) Average binding energy (|Ebind|) per light-metal atom doped to BeN2:2VBe monolayers (four metal atoms per 2×2 supercell), compared with the corresponding cohesive energy of the bulk metals. (b) Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations 4M@BeN2:2VBe (M = Li, Na, K), demonstrating structural stability and absence of metal clustering.

Figure 4. Spin-polarized electronic band structures and projected density of states (PDOS) of (a) 4Li@BeN2:2VBe, (b) 4Na@BeN2:2VBe, and (c) 4K@BeN2:2VBe monolayers. The high-symmetry M, K, and Γ points correspond to 0.3994, 0.6301, and 1.0913 Å−1, respectively. The Fermi energy (EF) is set to zero.

Figure 5. Charge density difference (CDD) maps showing the bonding characteristics of (a) 4Li@BeN2:2VBe, (b) 4Na@BeN2:2VBe, and (c) 4K@BeN2:2VBe monolayers. Yellow and cyan isosurfaces denote regions of charge accumulation and depletion, respectively (isosurface value: 0.005 e/Bohr3).
